It should therefore be valued, like all other European cultural expressions, which deserve to be protected and promoted globally.

It is a model that the world attempts to imitate. It is therefore essential to define its distinctive features and protect it, so as to prevent an entirely European heritage from being lost in the sea of globalization.

Everyone knows that my parliamentary group and the left in Europe have so far supported Turkey's accession process to the European Union, while simultaneously backing the reform process in that country.

The path to Turkey's accession to the Union runs through Diyarbakır and the DTP; otherwise, it will be we—those who have so far supported the accession process—who will be forced to request a pause for reflection that may prove definitive.

Thank you, Mr. President. The proposal on passengers' rights for bus travel aims to improve and protect passengers, particularly disabled individuals or those with reduced mobility.
This is the first regulation on bus passenger rights and should ensure passengers, as is already the case in the rail and air sectors.
The position in second reading aims to establish a series of rights for passengers who choose to travel by bus,

I agree with the rapporteur Cancian’s position, whom I thank for the excellent work carried out, and I believe the scope of application of the regulation should be revised by excluding regional services when they are integrated with urban and suburban services.
It would be fair and appropriate to ensure passengers' rights in the event of accidents, cancellations, or departure delays, as already decided by the Parliament in its first reading.

Equally important is the issue of passenger rights information. It would be beneficial, indeed, to provide passengers with information regarding connections with other modes of transport. This would also foster dialogue between passenger transport services by bus and those by rail.

With regard to disabled persons and those with reduced mobility, maximum protection must be ensured, and I would propose a 24-hour deadline instead of 48 to request assistance, as well as greater guarantees for compensation and replacement of mobility aids in the event of loss or damage.
Moreover, taking into account the needs of passengers with reduced mobility, any architectural barriers should be removed and existing infrastructure should be improved to ensure accessibility.
This regulation aims to enhance the competitiveness of the bus sector, promote intermodality, and above all, improve passengers' travel conditions.

Thank you, Mr. President. I thank the rapporteur, Sterks, for his excellent work on this report, which addresses how to extend free circulation in the maritime sector for navigation within the European Union.
I agree with the rapporteur on the objective of this directive, which is to exempt all maritime freight transport within the European Union from unnecessary administrative formalities.

Indeed, to achieve a European maritime space without borders, it is not only important that the various competent authorities cooperate satisfactorily, but also that the different simplification systems be implemented simultaneously.
This is necessary to ensure the proper functioning of the entire European borderless maritime area, making sea freight transport more attractive and enabling its optimal use.

Harmonizing administrative procedures among Member States will enable smoother navigation across the various ports of the European Union and will revive maritime freight traffic, which today accounts for less than 10% of the total.
To date, navigation continues to be hindered by the administrative formalities imposed upon it.
Electronic systems should therefore be activated as quickly as possible, as they would enable a faster data exchange system.

Moreover, interoperability is essential for achieving genuine administrative simplification. Indeed, the transmission of information via email is not entirely efficient and proves futile if information systems are not technically interoperable.
As for language, I believe that the use of a common language would undoubtedly benefit European maritime navigation,
In this way, communication would be more efficient, causing less confusion and fewer administrative delays.

Yes, thank you, Mr. President. First of all, I would like to offer my special congratulations to our rapporteur, Mr. Kramer, because we all know that we have truly achieved a result that at times seemed unattainable.

All of this, we must not conceal, has entailed considerable efforts, required sacrifices from all involved institutions, all political groups, and all national delegations, and has frequently placed us before truly difficult choices.

I am, however, here this evening to assert with conviction that, in my view, we have achieved the best possible outcome in safeguarding competition, the environment, and European industrial and employment policy.
On one hand, we have a directive imposing common obligations on all Member States, but on the other hand, we also have a directive that allows for the flexibility that is necessary.

I genuinely hope that the outcomes of this merger will immediately lead to a reduction in administrative burdens and, in the medium term, improved environmental performance and a strengthening of European industry.

European Council, which no one has voted for. Today we also have a rotating presidency in the hands of a government rejected by its own citizens.

The current crisis, however, should urge Europe to adopt ingredients fundamentally different from those that led to the failure.

A fundamental reconsideration is necessary, because the crisis is not a mirror of decline, but rather the death certificate of the European social model that Europopaganda would instead prescribe as a cure.

Mr. President, thank you, I apologize. I would like to emphasize in this intervention how the progressive erosion of European farmers' incomes, at the expense of growing

The profit margins within the agro-industrial supply chain are creating a concerning economic marginalization of those employed in the agricultural sector, resulting in the abandonment of these activities.
A shift in direction is therefore required, a fact acknowledged by the European Commission itself, and one reflected in the report currently under discussion in the Chamber this morning,
approval by the House this morning, which I wish to support, underscores with ample justification and suggestions, including legislative and sector-specific recommendations

Mobility and short-sightedness—this is the diagnosis of the malaise afflicting today's Europe 27.

Stagnation, because in Italy one in four young people is unemployed, and within the eurozone there are 16 million jobless—also because, despite the recent euro-enthusiasm, no concrete and effective measures have followed.

Myopia because, in outlining the way out of the crisis, Europe not only revived the usual recipe but also completely overlooked the most inherently territorial dimension of economic and social cohesion policy.

Only by placing the territory at the heart of political action, and by respecting its distinctive cultural vocations, can European development and employment policies be revitalized, drawing upon the inherent energies that each geographical area expresses—through districts, small and medium-sized enterprises, and craftsmanship.

To address the employment issue, one must first think on a small scale, invest in local institutions, and prioritize subsidiarity while safeguarding a heritage composed of linguistic and cultural diversity.
If the compass needle of European integration does not guide its policy toward the territory, the shipwreck of the Europroject is a certainty.
Thank you, Mr. President. In building an Europe of rights, a central role belongs to the freedom of information, the pluralism of information, and the independence of information.
Has the gentleman Commissioner, the lady Commissioner, noticed that there is an EU country—indeed one of the founding members through the Treaty of Rome—where there is no
the pluralism of information, where a Prime Minister directly or indirectly controls public and private television and print media.
What democratic electoral competition can we expect in a country where the Prime Minister and a political faction control the media?
What kind of democracy can exist in a country where a law is about to be passed shortly that prevents journalists from publishing facts related to judicial news?
Not Watergate, not a regime-friendly press, not a parlour poodle. What kind of democracy is it where the internet is to be brought under control?
What democratic country is it where those who oppose the dominant ideology are sidelined, and where free expression of thought and dissent are not guaranteed?

We are not describing Bocassa's Sudan, nor Ceausescu's Romania, nor Berlusconi's Italy, where unfortunately this occurs also because the European Commission lacks the courage to express an independent and free stance with respect to the member states.
The completion of the internal market for postal services by the end of this year is an important step, but it would be...

I furthermore believe that this directive, which already exists, should not be instrumentalized to justify the problem of social dumping.
It is the Member States that must implement the directive and they all have the necessary tools to do so.

the possibility for Libyan authorities to open fire on vessels if there is suspicion that they are transporting irregular migrants, because if that were the case among the provisions

women killed in 2009 in cases of domestic violence. A country where Christians live in fear of attacks by Islamic extremists. Just recall the brutal murder of Monsignor Padovanese.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, Commissioner, this measure aims to strengthen and rationalize the European Union's pharmacovigilance system.
These are the ambitious objectives set forth in the Meccheva reports, which respectively amend Regulation 726 and Directive 2183.

Thanks to intensive and efficient coordination work between the Council and the European Parliament, as well as among the relevant parliamentary committees with competence on the substance, it has been possible to
to discuss today a proposal aimed at improving the Community pharmacovigilance system and refining the substantial and decades-long activity of the European Union in this field.

Simplification, transparency, and rationalization can be considered the key elements of these two reports, which propose measures aimed at ensuring the highest possible protection of public health based on harmonized standards throughout Europe.

It refers, in fact, to a wide range of interventions ranging from a clearer definition of the roles and responsibilities of the main national and supranational stakeholders involved, to a new and more consciously active involvement of the patient, to whom one

recognizes a fundamental role for the actor within the system in strengthening the transparency of information regarding medicinal safety, and in simplifying the activities related to communication, registration, and assessment of suspected adverse effects.
The current pharmacovigilance system must be improved, also through closer collaboration at the European, national, and regional levels.
Effective participation at all three of these levels will therefore ensure a productive exchange of information regarding medicinal product side effects.
The new pharmacovigilance system will be capable of ensuring a high level of shared and secure information in favor of patients, national authorities, and healthcare professionals.
Thank you, Mr. President. Right now, here in Brussels, the events have taken place that

Many representatives from municipalities and regions discussed how to address the serious challenges facing urban life, as well as the old and new needs affecting workers, families, and small and large productive enterprises.
The economic crisis that has struck our societies has made it harder and more difficult for everyone to provide appropriate responses to a new, serious social issue.
In this context, I believe everyone understands that the cohesion policy has often been the only concrete tool available to regions for attempting to provide answers.
We can therefore say that, over recent years, cohesion policy has been an essential lever for the process of European integration for all regions.
We are now redefining this policy for the post-2013 period. We are therefore at a turning point.
For this reason, it is vital to avoid certain risks.
The first fundamental point is that of its renationalization. That would be a serious step backward.

Subsequently, it is necessary to ensure, in the construction of the funding budget, an adequate consideration both for those regions that are weaker and, at the same time, for the ability to avoid penalizing the more competitive and advanced regions.
Finally, it is essential to streamline procedures for those who have proven efficient, and to strengthen the capacity to impose sanctions on those who have not.
We expect the Commission to proceed in this direction.
Honourable colleagues, the path toward an Europe designed for women remains long and difficult.
The statistics speak clearly. The global crisis struck the labor market, severely testing female employment, which further declined by 0.7 percentage points in 2008.

Despite the recurring provocations on the topics of equal opportunities and employment that periodically fill a waning European propaganda, concrete measures to ensure women a genuine fulfillment both as workers and as mothers have not yet been adopted.
The Europe of the future needs to radically rethink its welfare model, not merely periodically change its label.

Indeed, the provision that aligns in Europe the Institute of maternity leave is positive; however, the choice to address paternal leave—complementary and equally important—only marginally within the same report is inefficient.

The greater integration of women is not only a moral value, but also a strategic objective for the sustainability of the much-publicized European social model, which continues to fail to convince me due to its lack of results.
Thank you, I will continue. Thank you, Mr. President. The rapporteur of the Development Committee, Mr. Mitchell, has asked me to substitute for him as he is unable to be present today.
I thank you for the work accomplished, as always timely and of high quality, with your proposals receiving, I would say, unanimous favorable feedback from the Development Committee.
Regarding what I will say, and above all regarding what my colleague Michel has achieved to date, I am convinced that the report can be adopted by a large majority.
But I now come to the report.
During 2009, the European Commission presented a proposal for amending Regulation 1905 of 2006, which establishes a financial instrument for development cooperation.
With this proposal, the Commission had asked Parliament to approve an amendment allowing non-governmental organizations to benefit from tax advantages when operating in developing countries.
proposed by us and accepted.
However, the Cooperation and Development Regulation also contains the implementing rules for the European Union's development policy.
These rules provide that when the Commission is required to allocate funding, it must follow comitology procedures.

This means that the Parliament has the authority to examine this proposed funding, and in the event that the Commission exceeds its competences, the Parliament may then adopt resolutions requesting the Commission to revise the decisions in question.

Since 2006, the Parliament has considered that the Commission had exceeded its executive powers in at least 12 cases, but in only three of these instances did the Commission actually modify or withdraw its proposal for decision.
Following the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, the Development Committee proposed applying the delegated acts procedure provided for in Article 290 of the Treaty of Lisbon.
The adoption of these procedures would mean that, at the very least in strategic funding decisions that the European Commission must take, the Parliament would have a more significant role.
Indeed, according to the Treaty of Lisbon, in certain well-defined cases, the Parliament may delegate to the Commission the power to take strategic decisions. But what might these decisions be?
In our view, the selection of countries to which the European Union should provide development assistance

It is a choice that falls within the competence of the legislator. Then, which sectors should be prioritized in funding? Education? Health? Environmental protection? The capacity for good governance? The development of small enterprises?
And how to ensure transparency in the management of development aid?
These are the areas, the subjects in which the European Parliament should play a more significant role than it has in the past.
It is here that the legislator must provide the executive with clear guidelines
And this is precisely the direction, I believe, toward which the demands of European citizens ultimately point.
I truly hope for as much support as possible for this report by Honourable Mitchell.
to the rapporteur on policy

The Commission will need to demonstrate concreteness by acting not only through governance tools, but also by implementing targeted legislative measures, drawing appropriate lessons from past mistakes, often caused by an overly sectoral approach.

participatory governance mechanisms, both at the inter-institutional level and in international cooperation for regional basins, including the Mediterranean, which stands out for the complexity of its jurisdiction.
It is precisely for this reason that the management of the Mediterranean basin must be understood as a shared heritage among all coastal states, based on the principle of

Conversely, the increasingly widespread practice of unilaterally declaring exclusive economic zones or similar areas in the Mediterranean Sea, in addition to being inconsistent with the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea, would undermine the concept of effective and shared governance.

Thank you, Mr. President. I thank Ms. Meissner for her report on integrated maritime policy. I agree with the rapporteur on the need to strengthen maritime transport and shipbuilding, to protect coastal regions, and to promote innovation and research activities.
Today, maritime transport plays a fundamental role in the commercial sector.
For this reason, cooperation between small and large ports facing the same sea basin should be encouraged, as they can facilitate a smoother commercial exchange.
Moreover, to ensure intermodality, investment is needed in port and hinterland infrastructure, particularly for ports that can be connected to European corridors.
Finally, I believe it is important not to overlook another issue, namely port security.

It is necessary to achieve homogenization of all security measures across all European ports, in order to better protect maritime transport and prevent unfair competition against those ports that, in order to ensure greater security, must incur higher costs.

Thank you, Mr. President. Thirty years ago, Morocco denied for many years that there was a war with the Sahrawi people. Today, it denies that there is disproportionate repression against the same people.
Yet in Western Sahara there are dead and wounded among a population that claims nothing more than the right to live in freedom on its own land.

No information and no deputies.

Europe watches passively.

Action must be taken against Morocco by halting all initiatives on trade agreements, starting with the renewal of fishing agreements.

Thank you, Mr. President. My intervention is not meant to be the usual rhetorical and cynical exercise, but rather a prompt that begins to focus more on substance than on form, and that abandons propaganda in favor of concreteness.
Having failed the Lisbon strategy, Europe has adopted the new EU 2020 slogan, but in practice has provided no innovative response to employment issues.

The data confirm this. The unemployment rate in Europe stands at 9.6, and 20.2 among young people. In Italy, the third net contributor to the EU budget, the situation for young people is even more dramatic, with 25.9 percent of youth unemployed.

In the Europe I would like to see, there are no waste of resources in promotional campaigns, but efficient investments so that training offers prepare professionals for sectors of the internal market, where there are concrete employment opportunities.
I conclude by emphasizing the importance of investing in professional training, a symbol of the excellence of our territories.
The world of craftsmanship offers unique employment potential and a valuable heritage of knowledge, which must be entrusted with the task of supporting the competitiveness of the European system.

Thank you, Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, and Madam President of the Council. The European commitment to setting ambitious goals ahead of the upcoming climate change conference is undoubtedly an objective to pursue with determination and awareness that the outcomes will shape the future of our planet.
But precisely for this reason, dear colleagues, we must be realistic and more cautious in our expectations.

The international negotiations leading up to the Cancun conference, so far as we know, have not only failed to achieve significant progress toward a global agreement, but have also reinforced distrust and resistance toward the commitments made in Copenhagen.
It has already been mentioned, but I would also like to mention China here.
China, for example, allows itself an increase in emissions of 5 billion tons of CO2 by 2020, with consequences that could potentially be offset
and perhaps not even that, but a mere attempt at compensation through a 100% reduction in European industrial emissions within the same year, by 2020.

This figure not only makes the proposal for an unconditional 30% reduction seem unrealistic, which we strongly oppose, but also leads many independent observers to hypothesize that global emissions will rise significantly by that date.

Here, in this contradictory situation, it would be at the very least risky, and even demagogic, to hypothesize figures and sketch suggestive but unrealistic scenarios in which the uncertainties inevitably reflect on the scale of costs.

The hope is that the European Union will bring to Cancun a shared, realistic, and sustainable proposal, because an agreement that delivers tangible and perceptible results is always and under any circumstances preferable to

expectations inevitably destined to failure and increasingly undermining credibility in the eyes of public and international opinion—this would indeed be a damage difficult to repair, the efforts of governments on major environmental issues.

Thank you, Mr. President. I have only one regret in this chamber this evening: that we are discussing the Sahrawis only because the Moroccans have invaded the Tindouf camp and the IUN. This is a regret for democracy within this Parliament.
The Parliament should have independently developed the will to address this issue, which has been stagnating on international tables for 30 years.
This is the most significant political fact.
We have been compelled by acts of violence, not because we voluntarily chose to uphold human rights.
Why, if not human rights, is it that a referendum on self-determination must take place there?
It is precisely because Morocco is our friend, because Morocco is the strong element in our relations. We must firmly and through all necessary actions demand an irrevocable will.

We must strive to create the conditions under which, Mr. President, the MINUSO mission, which is being closed, will not be left alone in establishing the conditions for a referendum that is continually postponed.
We must strive to use all the instruments available to the European Union to influence this event and ensure that that referendum delivers justice for thirty years of suffering endured by a people.

The report and discussion undoubtedly contain positive elements. The overall orientation is toward simplifying procedures and streamlining bureaucratic steps for third-country nationals holding a lawful residence permit in a Member State.

However, as a point for reflection, I would like to recall that today in Europe, tens of millions of citizens, including many young people, are unemployed due to structural shortcomings in the European productive system and as a result of a crisis that appears to have no end.

Before addressing the situation of third-country nationals residing in our countries, Europe must especially commit to implementing economic, political, and social measures that first help our citizens find or regain employment.
If Europe succeeds in providing employment for its citizens, it will establish solid social foundations for adequately managing migratory flows from outside.
The key principle must be, above all, to enable Europe to grow on its own legs and with its own strength.
our countries will then have the strength
Thank you, Mr. President. To date, access to employment is regulated only for certain specific categories of third-country nationals.
Indeed, not everyone benefits from the general principle of equal treatment in access to employment.

In this context, the European Union must act to ensure fair treatment for both third-country nationals lawfully residing within Member States and for beneficiaries of the status of refugee or person in need of international protection, pursuant to Directive 2004/83/EC of 29 April 2004.
It is necessary to avoid a growing demand for seasonal workers, for example, solely because they are paid less or have different, lower costs compared to a European citizen hired for the same job.

For example, one must avoid the risk that many multinational companies relocate their headquarters to countries such as Morocco or Turkey and send their employees to work in European branches because it is more cost-effective.
For reasons of social justice, ensuring equal treatment of European citizens in terms of remuneration, working conditions, and social security is essential.
For this reason, I consider it appropriate that seasonal workers, posted workers, refugees, and self-employed workers be included in this directive.
Making distinctions will become extremely dangerous.

Thank you, Mr. President. Distinguished colleagues, human trafficking, according to estimates by international organizations and police authorities, has now become the second-largest source of global profit for organized crime, after drug trafficking.
It is estimated that around 30 million people are victims of organ trafficking, prostitution, and forced labor.
This means that above all, the fight against this phenomenon of crime on such a vast scale is a battle for the security of our citizens, against clandestinity, against crime that generates crime.
Faced with a phenomenon of these proportions, Europe must rightly implement drastic measures against those who exploit human beings in various forms.

In combating this phenomenon, cooperation among member countries and law enforcement agencies is essential, but above all, the certainty of punishment for traffickers is crucial. Let us remember, however, that many individuals are already being detected.

exploited because in their countries of origin they find no dignified living conditions, living in poverty and despair, and are willing to do anything just to leave their homeland in pursuit of a better life that ultimately never materializes.

acceptable. Let us assist them in their own homes. This must be the guiding principle of European political action, so that our efforts are directed not only toward repression but also toward prevention.

Thank you, Mr. President, Lady Aston, esteemed colleagues. As Vice President of the Development Committee, I am engaged with the issues facing many countries, particularly in the African region, where human rights are regularly violated, trampled upon, and sometimes even forgotten.

such as female genital mutilation, also a serious violation of human rights and physical integrity of women.

enhancement of prevention, diagnosis, and refinement of available, including innovative, treatments, as well as the dissemination of information among Member States regarding progress achieved within each country in terms of public awareness of the disease.

Thank you, Mr. President. The situation is that Campania, particularly the city of Naples, have unfortunately become universally known for their night-time conditions, and the European Union has had to address this issue on multiple occasions.

If the emergency has struck Naples, while conditions elsewhere in Italy are very different—such as in my region, Veneto, where the collection of recyclables has been carried out for years without problems and with an extremely high recycling rate—it means that the responsibility for the emergency lies elsewhere.

The issue with the method is that we are proceeding through a device, for which we thank the honourable Fraga, namely oral question, to discuss something that should instead, following the Treaty of Lisbon, be a right of this Parliament.
I believe this practice must come to an end. We cannot resort to a strategy aimed at supporting a co-decision process.
Co-decision occurs when it is cultivated in the stages in which it matures.
Today we are holding this debate, it will conclude, the treaty will proceed, and in the end we will say yes or no.

Thank you, Mr. President. To date, the recommendations adopted by the General Fisheries Commission for the Mediterranean have been incorporated into Community legislation provisionally, through annual fishing opportunities regulations.
These concern conservation, management, exploitation, control, and marketing measures for fisheries and aquaculture products in the Mediterranean.
The permanent nature of this recommendation, however, requires a more stable legal instrument for their incorporation into Community law.
It is therefore appropriate to incorporate the aforementioned recommendations through a single legislative act, to which future recommendations may subsequently be added.
Today we have done this, and it will enhance legal certainty while simultaneously representing a significant step toward simplification.
At this point, the contracting parties of the CGMPM are obliged to ensure full implementation of agreed measures starting from the period of high stability.

A timely transposition was therefore necessary in order to make these international measures directly applicable to natural or legal persons at the community level and to ensure legal certainty of interest.

Mr. President, I believe the approval of our colleague Podima's report represents a significant step forward towards a more balanced, more advanced, and stronger economic and fiscal policy at the European level.

It is now evident to all the urgency of equipping ourselves, as Europe, as a coordination among the 27 countries, with economic and financial instruments capable of governing the economy rather than merely reacting to it.

The fact that the majority of this Parliament voted in favor of this provision is an important and significant development, as for the first time it did not simply let things slide, but instead took a step forward.

Thank you, Mr. President. The new Hungarian Constitution reduces the ability of oversight bodies, such as the Constitutional Court, to intervene and increases the powers of the executive, thereby undermining the normal balance of powers.
Social rights are being eroded; the state provides subsidies to the needy only if they agree to perform public service work.
Finally, the right to self-defense is established, allowing individuals to possess weapons at home even without a license,
The right to abortion is prohibited, and strongly discriminatory rules are introduced against homosexuals.
All of this contradicts Directive 78/2000, which establishes a general framework for combating discrimination.

I therefore ask the Commissioner how she intends to act, given that the fundamental rights established by the new Hungarian Constitution are only marginally compatible with those of the European Union and the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union.

Europe has established very strict controls, but these apply exclusively to fresh products.
No protective regulation has been issued for processed products, in particular for canned fish.
We import 370,000 metric tons of canned tuna. The main suppliers are countries in Southeast Asia, which import fish from Japan.

The Commission has recently acknowledged this issue and intends to address the gap through the reform of the common fisheries policy, which will not enter into force before two years, just as the Sommer report under discussion in this plenary session will not become effective before three years.
We must not create alarmism, but consumers have the right to urgent and transparent information,

The single market, free movement, and the Treaty of Lisbon are all elements that have come into effect regardless of any alteration to our manner of legislation, the European approach, or the national approach.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, Council Representative, and Commissioner, I would first like, as other colleagues have done, to positively welcome the anti-corruption package adopted by the European Commission on 6 June.
Corruption is a transnational phenomenon, and combating it requires European-scale measures.
The vast majority of our European citizens, according to Eurobarometer estimates, believe that Europe should do more.
How can they admit their wrongdoing?

In many member states, there has been a lack of political will to combat corruption. Consider that there are still European countries that have not signed the United Nations Convention against Corruption.

When we consider the devastating impact of corruption on democratic processes, on citizens' rights, and on trust in the rule of law, it is clear that the national anti-corruption measures adopted so far by Member States are insufficient to contain a phenomenon that is on the rise.

It is the European Union that must provide a unified and robust response, especially now, amidst an unprecedented economic crisis weighing heavily on people's lives, the sustainability of pension and welfare systems, and the capacity of institutions to ensure essential services.
Corruption causes the loss of 120 billion euros annually, equivalent to 1% of the European Union's GDP, and daily diverts resources from the Union and its member states that
They could be used to provide essential services, instructions, childcare, employment, transportation, in short, divert clean resources away from supporting people.

Therefore, greater courage and firmness are required, both in monitoring the implementation of anti-corruption measures and in intensifying and strengthening anti-corruption measures at the level of the Union.

In particular, we cannot speak of fighting corruption if we are unable to adopt at the very least minimum standards for defining the crime of corruption, a deficiency that is evident in the recently adopted package.
The same applies to the definition of uniform parameters for measuring the extent of the phenomenon across member countries, which is essential for preparing an effective response.
We support, for example, revitalizing the network of contact points between the two bodies against corruption, as I have proposed in the resolution we will vote on tomorrow.
The crisis is not new; it has existed for several years already.
Perhaps it is now time to conduct a critical assessment of the policies pursued so far. Was everything always done correctly?
We have always carried out appropriate analyses of the situation
Was the right approach chosen?
The same European Central Bank, which— as Minister Rostovski states— has saved Europe, has always acted appropriately, even when, fearing inflation, it raised interest rates.
President Barroso identifies a problem: the speed of markets and the slowness of politics. But is this the only problem?

Of course, if one considers that we are still in the implementation phase as of 21 July, this should at least suggest the need to better prepare and organize summits and their outcomes. But politics will always be slower than markets.
The point, then, is to look further ahead, shifting the emphasis toward the fight against unemployment and development,

This is the ground that the European Commission must choose. Even today, in President Barroso's speech, there was greater concreteness regarding the deficit and less on growth and development guidelines.

The Commission should overcome its weakness and clearly state that the priority must be fighting unemployment and creating jobs, redistributing wealth, and implementing a public investment strategy, while also changing the rules of the new European Stability Pact.
Mr. President, I believe the challenge of the coming years is to ensure that Europe, as has been stated, maintains its position of first rank.
a global tourist destination and, at the same time, be able to enhance, both in terms of environmental preservation and competitive capacity, its entire territorial heritage.

It is a challenging and far from guaranteed endeavor, especially considering not only the opportunities but also the aggressiveness of other countries in this sector, from other continents, ranging from China to India to Brazil.
Now, in this context, as the Commissioner Tajani recalled, it becomes essential, as also noted in the opinion of the Commission that we approved in the Regional Development Committee,
to combine a strong overarching strategy for tourism across the entire European Union with an appreciation of the role played by regions and territories.
In this sense, full involvement from the outset, in respect of the principle of subsidiarity, by the Regions and local authorities, is essential.
Moreover, I believe that the development of integrated tourism networks and projects should be encouraged, starting with the establishment of partnerships for the exchange of best practices.
I also consider appropriate the proposal to establish a Europe brand capable of defining high quality in the tourist product.
Of course, it will be up to the Commission to further define the priorities, particularly from a financial standpoint, to ensure that Europe's leadership is maintained in the future.

Thank you, Mr. President. European schools were established with the aim of ensuring access to education in one's mother tongue for the children of European Union officials called upon to work and live abroad in a cultural environment different from that of their country of origin.
The first European school was established in Luxembourg in 1953, initiated by a group of officials.
Over the years, given the positive outcomes of this teaching approach, a total of 14 European Schools have been established within the various Member States.

Although acknowledging that the European schools system represents a necessity rather than a luxury, it is evident that, more than 50 years after the establishment of the first European school, this system may exhibit outdated aspects and must therefore be appropriately updated and adapted to meet Europe’s new economic and social needs.

I therefore express my favourable vote on the text submitted by colleague Cavada, which aims at greater involvement by the European Union to support and coordinate the actions of Member States, called upon to work towards developing and improving the system of European schools.
And we all know that investing in education means investing in the future of our European youth. Thank you, Mr. President.

The numbers, if we look at them in their raw form, are very telling. The number of disasters worldwide has quintupled. If we consider that in 1975
Seventy-eight catastrophic events had been recorded, whereas today we face nearly 400 disastrous events per year.

Over the past 20 years, unfortunately, the victims of these disasters have amounted to 90,000, more than 29 million people have been affected by disasters, and economic losses have reached 211 billion euros.
It is clear that we are facing phenomena of such magnitude that demand a stronger response, a more effective response, and a truly strong commitment from us.
We all have in our eyes the images of the earthquake in Haiti, the floods in Pakistan, and what happened in Japan.
Europe itself witnesses sudden floods and wildfires each year on its territory; we recall the ash cloud following the eruption of the Icelandic volcano.

An oppressed ethnic minority, Christians forced to secretly live out their faith, priests brutally killed with no action taken by the authorities. This is today's Turkey: an anti-Western foreign policy.
My party, the Northern League, has always been opposed to Turkey's entry into Europe, as it represents a potential Trojan horse for radical Islamism on our continent.
Today more than ever, we are convinced that the EU must put an end to the accession negotiations.
The intransigent threats that Turkey has issued against Europe must receive an immediate response.
One cannot accept that a third country dictates to Europe what it must do.
Cyprus is a full-fledged member state of the EU and indisputably has the right to assume the rotating presidency.
Europe must send a clear signal and demonstrate its readiness to defend Cyprus by every possible means against Turkey's political and military arrogance and aggression.
We immediately halt the accession negotiations and remain prepared to also suspend commercial relations.
Mr. President, we are experiencing a decisive turning point for the future of the euro and, with it, for the entire European project.
Having this awareness would mean moving beyond a myopic logic that subordinates everything to an ideological insistence on budgetary balance.
We all want the accounts of various states to be in order, we all want to reduce indebtedness.
However, it is clear that without an intelligent investment policy capable of revitalizing the economy, there will be no possibility of growth.
The only clear outcome in this setup is that heavier prices, as always, will be borne by the most vulnerable social classes.
For this reason, together with the group of Socialists and Democrats, we voted against this report.
Indeed, one continues in a single direction, that of rigor without growth, a direction that has proven incorrect thus far.
Highly appreciated is the work carried out by colleague Ferrera in this report.
Indeed, among the package of six legislative proposals concerning economic governance, only on this one did I and my group cast a positive vote.

We did so because, among other things, a clause safeguarding social rights through collective bargaining was included, and furthermore, the role of Parliament in adopting delegated acts has been strengthened.

Thus, although we cannot alter the overall balance of the package of six proposals, this report undoubtedly represents a step forward, particularly in establishing a surveillance system that takes fundamental rights into account.

And thus, in this context, and notwithstanding our view that a strategy based solely on austerity does not yield positive results, this report undoubtedly represents a positive note against the prevailing trend.

Honourable colleagues, Finland and the Netherlands have raised this issue in Council for a very simple reason. Bulgaria and Romania are currently unable to ensure effective border control, and are plagued by rampant corruption involving the political class and organized crime.
It is not the populist movements that are cited, but the airport in its 2010 report on organized crime.
There is no populist propaganda, but a serious security issue.

Should Bulgaria join this moment, police and security agencies foresee that Bulgarian borders, particularly the maritime ones, would become an easy target for Turkish and Eastern European crime, and Bulgaria and Romania would become a crossroads for human and drug trafficking.

It is natural that some Member States have significant reservations about Bulgaria and Romania's actual ability to ensure effective border controls against organized crime and corruption, and it is therefore entirely natural that they have opposed their accession to Schengen.
Allowing this now would pave the way for transnational crime and create a breach in European borders, precisely at a time when there is intense migratory pressure from Turkey.
The reasons for opposing are many, and Europe cannot ignore them.
I therefore wish to know the intentions of the Commission and the Council regarding this delicate matter, which in my opinion has so far been handled with
and without taking into account the risks that the accession of Bulgaria and Romania would entail, according to competent bodies dealing with security and international crime.

In my opinion, the Commission and the Council should postpone the accession of these two countries to Schengen and should only give approval when there is reasonable assurance that the accession of Bulgaria and Romania will not have repercussions on the security of European citizens.

Thank you, Mr. President. The crisis in Europe is entangling public finances and private capital issues in a vicious cycle with the productivity challenges facing our businesses. The prospect of recession is real.
If therefore the priority given to SMEs is crucial, I ask the Commission that, together with Parliament, we must absolutely ensure that our sound principles are put into
practice, and that the legislative initiatives of the SBA, the Small Business Act, become concrete practice among Member States, with a high level of effectiveness and quality.

Consider, Mr. Commissioner, what would not be achieved if all Member States accelerated the implementation of the directive on payment delays? What a significant advantage this would be for SMEs! Therefore, SME evidence is important, but we are still at the methodological level.

Of course, it is right to verify whether, when we legislate, we are truly doing so in the interest of and with due consideration for small and medium-sized enterprises; however, we are asking that progress be made with great consistency and determination, indeed with depth, in establishing the framework of opportunities that you have outlined.
If we value what is also being done by the Commission, we are ready—budgetarily and through the other means at our disposal—to support it.
I will say just one word about credit.
Let's have a clear conversation with the banks, I'm saying this to you, Commissioner, because we must support small and medium-sized enterprises.

without prejudicing the usual avenues of access to justice, I am convinced that an alternative dispute resolution (ADR) offers a swift and cost-effective alternative to legal litigation.

concretized, on the one hand, by avoiding setting unrealistic and unattainable requirements, and on the other hand, by establishing a so-called flexibility regime, articulated through the provision of time periods and corresponding quantitative limits of

Honourable Tantarella, the work accomplished has been significant, as you have managed to carefully balance the demands arising from the major challenges facing the European Union at this time with those related to environmental protection and the current economic crisis.
Thank you, Mr. President. The current global economic crisis, even more severe than that of 1929, has caused unsustainable imbalances in international economies over the past few years.
Commercial imbalances among countries, financial markets lacking common rules, and instability in banking systems are all potential threats to financial and economic stability.
This resolution, to which I express my support, urgently underscores the need for informed citizenship, with rules and global economic coordination.

The economy requires a global governance system, open markets, high economic and social standards, more responsible monetary policy, and stricter supervisory policies. And decision-making bodies, international economic organizations, must be the key actors at the heart of this new approach.

Europe specifically needs a transparent framework to reduce exacerbated imbalances, further intensified by globalization, and to ensure broad-reaching reforms aimed at promoting growth and reducing the debt of individual states.
This is the commitment, and this resolution underscores it for all countries.

Thank you, Mr. President. I voted in favor of the report by my colleague, Honourable Matera, which examines the situation of lone mothers, who today, as already stated, represent more than 5% of the population of the European Union.

healthcare, flexible work opportunities enabling the balance of private and professional life, priority access to affordable housing, and special unemployment benefits.

Mr. President, the European Union has produced an enormous amount of legislation and regulations to build European law, and has created the European area known as the Stockholm Programme.
There is a discrepancy between this body of legislation, this legislative monument, and the extent to which citizens utilize it, which is numerically and proportionally very low.

On the other hand, national judges and national lawyers of the individual Member States have grown up within the legal system of their respective countries and have predominantly received training in this context.
While we believe that the true European judge is the national judge who changes his mindset and becomes imbued with an understanding of European law.

For this reason, before discussing training, it is essential to engage in extensive communication with citizens and legal professionals, so that they become advocates of this major innovation and help foster the willingness to take advantage of the benefits offered by this right.

But above all, it must be a bottom-up movement that develops within in-service training. One cannot deliver lectures to adult, highly professional individuals in judicial training as if they were schoolchildren.

One must stimulate curiosity when dealing with one's own cases by connecting with other colleagues and seeking solutions, as today Common Law and Civil Law converge and are no longer as distant from each other as they once were within legal culture.

This is the crucial point. In our proposals—and I am pleased that the European Commission has come to recognize these aspects—we, in our Convention, call for a reversal of trend, bottom-up, placing greater emphasis on legal studies rather than judicial training.
There is an example in the Netherlands called Eurinfra, very interesting: it is a network of officials in each court who disseminate this knowledge and encourage the judges in this regard.
Here, the essence of our project is this; we hope it may receive full support from the Commission.

As envisaged in the Lisbon Treaty, the European Parliament has become a full-fledged institutional subject in the field of security policies and therefore has the right to actively and decisively participate in shaping the priorities of the internal security strategy and the Union's model of security.

Mr. President, with the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, the foundations have been laid for the development of a penal law integrated within community law.

While recognizing the importance of mutual recognition and the harmonization of protection standards, it is essential to emphasize that the law must be extremely clear and easily understandable, so that it is evident to everyone which actions constitute a crime and which do not.
This particularly applies to directives that must be mutually compatible with national law.
Any lack of clarity, indeed, could give rise to divergent interpretations of the law.

I therefore believe that only a close collaboration among European institutions, based on shared principles and methods, can ensure a coherent, high-quality European strategy in the field of criminal law.
Thank you, Mr. President. The European Union safeguards the general interests of consumers against unfair business practices, misleading advertising, and abusive contractual terms.

I therefore express my favourable vote on the colleague Perez's report because I agree that the creation of a strategy for consumer law
the so-called most vulnerable, not only contribute to their social inclusion, but also help ensure a single market that is safer, fairer, and more competitive.

a strategy aimed at protecting the rights of the most vulnerable consumers, in view of the strategy that the European Commission and Member States will be called upon to develop in this regard.

Thank you, thank you, Mr. President. The management of bluefin tuna fishing has a long, rather complex history dating back to the 1990s and is today probably the most strictly regulated by the European Union.

Thank you, Mr. President. The road is still long, but today may be an important step toward the eventual introduction and implementation of a tax on financial transactions.
I understand the prudence, caution, and need for gradualism expressed by Commissioner Scemeta, as well as the technical issues rightly highlighted by Minister Vestager.

But precisely for this reason, I hope the Parliament will express a clear political will, with a broad majority, to provide momentum and impetus to the necessary initiatives aimed at addressing the issues that remain unresolved, particularly at the level of the Council.
We all want to carefully assess the impact of this tax, but it is unfair to raise unwarranted fears, which may lead to contradictory and counterproductive decisions.

If one is attentive to reducing the tax's impact, then it is important to have the broadest possible tax base with a low rate, rather than seeking to extend exemptions indefinitely.

Thank you, Mr. President. Since 1990, through the instrument of macrofinancial assistance, the European Union has provided economic aid to countries experiencing short-term balance of payments difficulties in the form of grants, loans, or a combination of both.

Thank you, Mr. President. Approximately 100 million European citizens are engaged in various forms of volunteer activities.

From major sporting events to cultural gatherings, volunteers play a crucial role, and one must not overlook the work of those who rush to provide assistance and solidarity in the event of severe natural or environmental disasters.
Volunteering represents one of the broadest expressions of active citizenship and a crucial activity for the development of democratic values and equality within the European Union.
For this reason, I look forward to the development of high-quality national and cross-border volunteering and to the elimination of existing obstacles.
Thank you, Mr. President. From communications to finance, transportation, energy, culture, and healthcare, technology's role is increasingly significant.
The increasingly frequent use of the Internet and digital tools makes the issue of cybersecurity one of the top priorities on the European agenda.

Information and communication technologies can indeed promote economic and social development only if existing regulations ensure user security with regard to data confidentiality and intellectual property rights.

For this reason, only full international cooperation involving states, members, institutions, and industry stakeholders can give rise to a robust system capable of implementing effective measures to ensure cybersecurity.
Thank you, Madam President, thank you, Commissioner; you have said important things, very much worth agreeing with.

all our efforts, as they will suffocate the companies themselves with new bureaucratic burdens, devastating economic losses, and new forms of unfair competition.

and we have recorded a strong unity between Parliament and the Commission on this.

There has been an initiative and activism by multiple countries, not to create new exclusive alliances, but to build a shared consensus respectful of all member states.
This is the path toward the political objective of unity, without which any banking, fiscal, and monetary union will be entirely inadequate.
In June, with the Council's decision to restore to Member States the possibility of reinstating border controls, a new Europe was born—a Europe courageous in fulfilling the desire of
Greater security for its citizens, finally inspired by a more mature understanding of the concept of solidarity, brought back to the defense of rights, above all those of its peoples.

To President Schulz, even though he is not present, I say that this historic decision does not represent an interinstitutional incident, but rather a respect for the will of the citizens—and not for that eurobureaucracy which for years has pretended not to understand what is truly happening beyond the glass palace.

Science gives life to our land, civilization, and well-being, and for a moment we may aspire to once again become a capital of world science.

Thank you, Mr. President. Victims of crime within the European Union too often refrain from filing complaints due to fear or lack of trust in institutions.

all Member States, paying particular attention to those victims who are at high risk of suffering further harm or intimidation during the criminal proceedings.

On September 11, the Catalan people peacefully demonstrated in Barcelona.

In Veneto, my region, in recent weeks thousands of people have signed in order to hold a referendum on Veneto's independence from Italy.
Yesterday, in Veneto, Lombardy, and all the regions of northern Italy, hundreds and thousands of people signed petitions demanding greater autonomy from Rome.
Yet even in the Basque Country, the citizens are loudly demanding autonomy and independence from the nation-states.

It calls for a courageous commitment to clearly say no to all separatist tendencies.
His deputy, Vidal Quadras, went even further, openly calling for the use of force against Catalan citizens.
Their statements constitute a most serious act, contrary to the principles of the democracy which here we preach and which we must defend.
I tell them that it will be the peoples who will write the future of the new Europe, not the bureaucrat in Brussels.
The path is already marked.
The strict economic policy adopted to overcome the crisis has not yielded the expected results.
as highlighted in the report by colleague Lavia, only by investing in a more selective manner and supporting the ideal of a European budget aimed at
Sustaining growth and employment will make it possible to rebalance the economies of the Member States, thereby improving the situation of European citizens.

Thank you, Mr. President. The social and economic context in which the multiannual financial framework for the 2014–2020 period is being discussed is rather problematic.

With member states committed to implementing fiscal adjustments within their respective national budgets to address public finances, the European Union is viewed in some cases as an entity that exacerbates the financial burden on taxpayers.

For this reason, to truly overcome the crisis and secure a better future for young Europeans, it will be of fundamental importance to support increased and more concrete investments in the fields of training and development.
This is the strategic charter of an institution. From the budget one can understand what it is, what it does, and above all, what it aims to achieve in the future.

We face a crucial choice: between a survival budget, a mere dragging along, perhaps a little less burdensome for everyone, or a budget of courage, of selective choices, of investments in employment, innovation, and young people—indeed, in the future of the European Union and its vocation for development.

Everyone should understand that, as colleague Calfin clearly stated among others, Europe can today accomplish what individual states can no longer achieve for the economy, regional development, and territories, where social cohesion either establishes or undermines the lives of the citizens we have discussed.

Today, Europe's major decisions risk being stifled by accounting disputes within the Council, not only among beneficiary and contributing countries, but also between net contributor and recipient nations.

We argue that this is a false problem, because it is easy to demonstrate with figures and numbers—not with words—what has occurred in the past, that contributing countries, sometimes more than others, benefit from economic convergence and growth.
The Council is currently working to assign figures to the various headings.

The war of positions has begun, with statements and positions taken by certain states that regard the budget as just any other expenditure, one that can be reduced without remorse, indeed perhaps useful in nurturing an easy-to-activate anti-European sentiment during times of economic crisis.
It is a mistaken position that does not aid the integration we are striving to achieve in other areas.
Dear colleagues, our Europe remains incomplete and mutilated, as we have often stated.
We have adopted a stability pact, which was also meant to foster development, but we have stabilized so much that we are now in recession.
Here, let us at least seize the opportunity offered by this budget, this multiannual framework, to make choices in the right direction.
Thank you, Mr. President. The lack of information and awareness regarding one's rights, compounded by obstacles
Language barriers and the non-recognition of diplomas and qualifications are the most common problems encountered by EU citizens when residing, studying, or working in another European Union country.
Unfortunately, there remains a lack of visibility regarding the concept of Union citizenship and its tangible benefits.
For this reason, I have expressed my support for 2013 as the European Year of Citizens.

The primary objective is therefore to strengthen awareness of the rights associated with EU citizenship, and to assist citizens in fully exercising their right to free movement and residence within the territories of the Member States.
Mr. President, the growing number of innovative financial instruments poses numerous challenges in terms of regulation, governance, and monitoring their effectiveness.
A proper balance must indeed be found between the objectives of transparency and oversight, as well as a sufficient level of effectiveness and speed of implementation.

The introduction of such instruments at the European level is nonetheless of great importance, as it represents a means through which the European Union, particularly during times of crisis, can stimulate investment in the economy.

Brussels I Regulation has undoubtedly represented one of the most effective instruments
of Union legislation, as it has laid the foundations for a European judicial area promoted and ensured in the certainty of law among European citizens.

on the jurisdiction over disputes involving parties domiciled in third countries and the agreements concerning the choice of competent forum.
I am convinced that the adoption of the new regulation can ensure stronger protection for consumers, greater legal certainty within the internal market, and for this reason I have voted in favor.

Mr. Commissioner, today you have called for Europe to assume a new role in coordinating industrial policies, and even more stringent advocates of autonomy and private initiative now demand the exercise of this function.
Without a greater European integration of national strategies, even in terms of employment protection, the current crisis will not be overcome and there will be no revival.
The crisis in the automotive sector is not merely one of overproduction capacity; it is a dramatic loss of employment.
But the crisis now appears structural and concerns the very fate of the car, of the product, as it exists today.
We want to secure the automobile industry in Europe and we want to avoid dispersing existing expertise, know-how, and professional skills,
We therefore call for the initiation of a major restructuring and revitalization project, and we urge that it be carried out promptly, as costs are proportional to time.
Restructuring cannot be merely the result of fragmented national decisions or individual firms.
At the very least, we must attempt to coordinate and steer this painful process at the community level, bringing the relevant parties together around the table through social dialogue.
producers, trade unions, and the governments of individual countries, offering the support of research, innovation, human capital development, reskilling, and the necessary funding.
I know it will be a difficult undertaking because the national interests of individual companies are not aligned.

But since the current competitive struggle within the European Union is highly ambiguous and sometimes risks being unfair, I believe that, in the end, it is in the interest of all countries to have effective community-driven impetus and leadership to jointly confront the increasingly aggressive global market.

The energy crisis has imposed and continues to impose a very high cost on all employment, affecting thousands of precarious workers as well as stable and temporary workers, who are numerous in this sector.
The major steel conglomerates are increasingly shifting toward the Far East or the American continent, raising a significant question about the direction of our development model.
I believe Europe must regain the understanding that a manufacturing system such as ours requires robust basic production capable of supplying the European internal market.
We must therefore defend this sector, the role of a stable steel industry; we will support it, Mr. Commissioner, if we wish to halt industrial decline.

And I believe we must move beyond a dualism that has not been reached here this evening, but which often surfaces in discussions on this subject—a dualism between environmental advocates and workers' interests—which is incorrect because it prevents us from envisioning a future perspective.
An industry that regards steelmaking as an integral part must rely on advanced production systems and concrete, balanced solutions that safeguard diverse interests and differing needs.

Therefore, we need strong, even European-level, strategic planning focused on sustainability, which, in my view, we must not retreat from, as it represents a valuable added advantage with which we wish to compete globally, supporting investments, as already mentioned.

As stated by various sources, new technologies and new processes for the requalification of facilities are required for an European economy that makes effective use of resources, including energy resources, indeed starting from energy resources, as well as the research into new and different materials.

Yes, thank you, Mr. President. As we have recalled in this Chamber, next December the United Nations agency dedicated to information and communication technologies will convene in Dubai for the World Conference on International Communications.
The subject is the renegotiation of international regulations on telecommunications.

We cannot forget, and I am pleased by the words of Commissioner Tajani regarding the work accomplished to date, which cannot be called into question; nor can we allow the authority of the European Union to be bypassed by another authority.

a supranational organization such as the ITU, of which we know hundreds of states from around the world are members, many of which are known for issues related to democracy, transparency, and freedom of expression.
Therefore, it is not incorrect to consider the pillars of the Internet—freedom, participation, openness, innovation—as being at risk.

Therefore, I believe it is necessary to safeguard the role of the Internet and the potential that the Internet, above all, can have in revitalizing economic growth, not merely that, but also in ensuring opportunities for everyone.
I therefore hope that the Commission works towards the goal of uniting Europe and Dubai, enabling them to speak with one voice.

Many controversial proposals have been presented at the negotiating table of the World Conference on International Telecommunications. In particular, those suggesting changes to Internet governance, which raise concerns about undermining network neutrality with negative implications for consumers.
Therefore, Internet freedom is at risk, or more precisely, the freedom of expression and data protection.
Indeed, what some states have proposed would allow a government not only to intrude into the communications of its own citizens but even into the data flowing across its own networks.
The step from here to the limitation of freedom of expression and censorship is extremely short, and therefore I believe it is in contradiction with the fundamental principles of the European Union.

Thank you, Mr. President. I believe today must be the opportunity to objectively discuss the important agreement that Kazakhstan is negotiating with the European Union.
Kazakhstan is a crucial country regarding the stability and strategic future of Central Asia and is undoubtedly an important economic and commercial partner of the European Union.
It is a country that has already initiated economic reforms and also a democratic process, but still needs to make considerable efforts to fully complete these processes.

It is, however, a country that with this objective continues to demonstrate its readiness for new and more solid reforms, and I believe this is precisely the role that the European Union should play in supporting them.

For this reason, I am certain that signing this enhanced agreement will enable forward progress, as its approval will strengthen an already solid and productive relationship and ensure continuous support from the European Union.

I remain convinced, and I believe this should have been emphasized even more in the report we will vote on tomorrow, that progress toward democracy and human rights must be accompanied by a renewed, open, and constructive mutual commitment.

Our objective must not be to adopt a negative stance toward these and other countries in view of the immediate fulfillment of changes. Our objective must be to support the efforts of these countries in achieving the goal, including the respect for the principles of human rights.

The report in fact highlights a number of advantages, the situation of a minimal amount allocated to small-scale fishing from the European Fund.

Sector. We have discussed this issue several times in the Committee. Because if not, not only will fishing die out, probably due to the depletion of seas and fish stocks, but it will also die out because young people are no longer drawn to this sector.

Although this practice has been prohibited since 2003 in European waters and for the European fleet, the Commission has decided to submit a proposal aimed at eliminating even the exemption allowing the removal of shark fins aboard Union fishing vessels.

This proposal, by further ensuring the protection that sharks require, will enable the European Union to actively contribute to the global effort underway to eradicate the cruel practice of shark finning in other parts of the world.
